structured.summary: This paper proposes a meta-framework for differentially private fine-tuning of large-scale pre-trained language models using parameter-efficient methods such as LoRA, adapters, and Compacters. The approach achieves state-of-the-art privacy-utility tradeoffs on various NLP tasks, demonstrating that larger models better retain accuracy under privacy constraints while reducing computational and memory overhead.

strengths.point: Introduces a meta-framework leveraging parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods for differentially private training, which simplifies and improves private fine-tuning.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.1 and 3.2 describe the meta-framework and its instantiations using LoRA, adapters, and Compacters.

strengths.point: Achieves state-of-the-art privacy-utility tradeoffs, with private models approaching non-private accuracy on multiple NLP benchmarks.
strengths.evidence: Section 4.1 reports results on GLUE tasks where private RoBERTa-Large achieves 87.8% accuracy on MNLI with ε=6.7, close to the non-private 90.2%.

strengths.point: Demonstrates that larger models maintain better accuracy under differential privacy, which contrasts with prior assumptions about noise scaling.
strengths.evidence: Section 4.1 and 4.2 show improved performance for RoBERTa-Large vs. RoBERTa-Base and GPT-2-Large vs. smaller GPT-2 models under DP.

strengths.point: Reduces computational and memory costs by updating only a small fraction of parameters, enabling practical private fine-tuning of large models.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.3 and experimental details in Section 4 highlight reduced parameter updates (0.05% to 1%) and improved training speed and memory usage.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of hyperparameter sensitivity and tuning strategies for full fine-tuning under DP.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.1 notes that full fine-tuning with DPSGD was not fully optimized due to resource constraints, leaving open whether it can match parameter-efficient methods.

weaknesses.point: Experiments focus primarily on English NLP tasks and standard benchmarks, limiting assessment of generalization to other languages or domains.
weaknesses.evidence: All experiments are conducted on GLUE tasks and E2E NLG dataset, with no evaluation on multilingual or domain-specific datasets.

weaknesses.point: Theoretical understanding of why parameter-efficient methods work better under DP is not deeply explored.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.2.4 mentions intrinsic dimensionality as a hypothesis but does not provide empirical or theoretical analysis in the DP context.

weaknesses.point: Privacy accounting relies on recent advanced methods but the impact of different privacy parameters (ε, δ) on utility is not extensively studied.
weaknesses.evidence: Privacy budgets are fixed or chosen based on prior work; no ablation or sensitivity analysis on privacy parameters is presented.

weaknesses.point: The paper does not discuss potential limitations or risks related to privacy leakage from the public pre-training data.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 1 briefly mentions privacy concerns about pre-training data but does not analyze their implications or mitigation.

weaknesses.point: No comparison with other recent parameter-efficient tuning methods such as prefix-tuning or prompt-tuning under DP.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5 acknowledges other methods but does not empirically evaluate or compare them.

weaknesses.point: The computational cost and resource requirements for reproducing experiments, especially on large models, are high and may limit accessibility.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.1 mentions inability to run full DPSGD fine-tuning on RoBERTa-Large due to resource constraints.

questions.question: Can the authors provide more insights or empirical analysis on why parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods maintain accuracy better under DP compared to full fine-tuning?
questions.motivation: Understanding the underlying reasons could guide development of even more effective private training algorithms.

questions.question: How sensitive are the results to the choice of privacy parameters ε and δ? Have the authors explored tradeoffs at different privacy budgets?
questions.motivation: Evaluating utility across a range of privacy levels would help practitioners choose appropriate settings.

questions.question: Have the authors considered or tested their methods on multilingual or domain-specific datasets to assess generalizability?
questions.motivation: Real-world applications often require privacy-preserving models beyond English and standard benchmarks.

questions.question: Could the authors comment on the potential privacy risks associated with the public pre-training data, and whether their approach mitigates any leakage from this stage?
questions.motivation: Clarifying this would strengthen the privacy guarantees and practical applicability of the method.

critique_points.point_001: Limited exploration of hyperparameter sensitivity and tuning strategies for full fine-tuning under DP. (Section 4.1 notes that full fine-tuning with DPSGD was not fully optimized due to resource constraints, leaving open whether it can match parameter-efficient methods.)
critique_points.point_002: Experiments focus primarily on English NLP tasks and standard benchmarks, limiting assessment of generalization to other languages or domains. (All experiments are conducted on GLUE tasks and E2E NLG dataset, with no evaluation on multilingual or domain-specific datasets.)
critique_points.point_003: Theoretical understanding of why parameter-efficient methods work better under DP is not deeply explored. (Section 3.2.4 mentions intrinsic dimensionality as a hypothesis but does not provide empirical or theoretical analysis in the DP context.)
critique_points.point_004: Privacy accounting relies on recent advanced methods but the impact of different privacy parameters (ε, δ) on utility is not extensively studied. (Privacy budgets are fixed or chosen based on prior work; no ablation or sensitivity analysis on privacy parameters is presented.)
critique_points.point_005: The paper does not discuss potential limitations or risks related to privacy leakage from the public pre-training data. (Section 1 briefly mentions privacy concerns about pre-training data but does not analyze their implications or mitigation.)
critique_points.point_006: No comparison with other recent parameter-efficient tuning methods such as prefix-tuning or prompt-tuning under DP. (Section 5 acknowledges other methods but does not empirically evaluate or compare them.)
critique_points.point_007: The computational cost and resource requirements for reproducing experiments, especially on large models, are high and may limit accessibility. (Section 4.1 mentions inability to run full DPSGD fine-tuning on RoBERTa-Large due to resource constraints.)
critique_points.point_008: Can the authors provide more insights or empirical analysis on why parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods maintain accuracy better under DP compared to full fine-tuning? (Understanding the underlying reasons could guide development of even more effective private training algorithms.)
critique_points.point_009: How sensitive are the results to the choice of privacy parameters ε and δ? Have the authors explored tradeoffs at different privacy budgets? (Evaluating utility across a range of privacy levels would help practitioners choose appropriate settings.)
critique_points.point_010: Have the authors considered or tested their methods on multilingual or domain-specific datasets to assess generalizability? (Real-world applications often require privacy-preserving models beyond English and standard benchmarks.)
critique_points.point_011: Could the authors comment on the potential privacy risks associated with the public pre-training data, and whether their approach mitigates any leakage from this stage? (Clarifying this would strengthen the privacy guarantees and practical applicability of the method.)
